chapterbody 19: This answer “to repay karma” cannot be simpler and clearer! “Karma” is incomprehensible. “Karma” is the cause of reincarnation.
chapterbody 22: Therefore, I say:
chapterbody 24: Birth is from karma.
chapterbody 25: Death follows karma.
chapterbody 26: Life is to repay karma.
chapterbody 28: Let me simply explain to everyone:
chapterbody 30: When one is copulating, one’s mind is in the animal realm.
chapterbody 31: When one has hateful thoughts of killing, one’s mind is in the hell realm.
chapterbody 32: When one has a compassionate heart to help other people, one’s mind is in the heaven realm.
chapterbody 33: When all the karma one has committed are neither very kind nor very evil, then one’s mind is in the human realm.
chapterbody 34: When one is jealous of someone else’s accomplishments and looking for ways to strike someone down, then one’s mind is already in the asura realm.
chapterbody 35: When one’s behavior towards anyone or any matters is stingy, then one’s mind is in the hungry ghost realm.
chapterbody 36: Of course, these are just simple examples.
chapterbody 40: Reincarnation is inconceivable. To where one will reincarnate depends on the result of all one’s karmas after being added, subtracted, multiplied, divided, or the combination of all these factors. chapterbody: hile in Taiwan, I once met two Buddhist disciples in an elevator. They were both around 60 years old, dressed in monk-like robes, a top with cloth buttons and long pants.
chapterbody 2: This type of clothing resembled that of a Kung Fu style outfit. The color was gray.
chapterbody 3: They recognized me.
chapterbody 4: They said, “Living Buddha Lu, you have come back to Taiwan!”
chapterbody 5: I said, “Yes,” and then I asked, “Which practice are you cultivating?” They answered, “The practice of mind-observation.”
chapterbody 6: I asked, “Is the practice of mind-observation a meditation?” They were bewildered and unable to answer.
chapterbody 10: As far as I know, the term meditation can be called by many different words, which leaves a lot of room for confusion. Many cultivators are unable to make heads or tails of it.
chapterbody 12: We often see many different special terms used but there is no further in depth understanding of its meaning.
chapterbody 14: I am now going to especially explain the following:
chapterbody 16: Samatha--This sounds like samadhi, but it is not the same. This is of not allowing the mind of any thoughts. The idea is to have a cessation of breathing and cessation of thoughts to truly let go completely into emptiness.
chapterbody 18: Samapatti--This namely is to use the power of cessation to practice observation, like the “practice of mind-observation,” which uses cessation [of wandering thoughts] to practice observation.
chapterbody 20: Samapanna--At this level, the mind is concentrated without any wandering thoughts. Observation of the truth becomes clear. The focus is in the extreme.
chapterbody 22: Dhyana--is the state of conscious mind. It encompasses the effort of all types of meditation including the three mentioned previously. Some people may say that dhyana includes everything from the first meditation heaven to the attainment of arhatship.
chapterbody 24: Samadhi--This is the highest level and is also the state when the dharma body’s absolute observation and Tathagata’s perfect enlightenment merges as one.
chapterbody 26: It is the same as the Vajrayana Buddhism’s child light entering into the mother light. The two bond tightly and merge as one.
chapterbody 28: Symbolized as “one.”
chapterbody 29: Also is “zero.”
chapterbody 30: This is the supreme perfect enlightenment of “samadhi.”
chapterbody 34: As to the so-called terms “da zhuo (meditation),” “jing zhuo (quietly sitting),” “chan ding (Zen meditation),” “zhuo chan (meditative sitting),” and etc., they are all ambiguous.
chapterbody 36: So I say:
chapterbody 39: “Confusing.”
chapterbody 40: “Not clear.”
chapterbody 42: In the Great Treatise on the Perfection of Wisdom, it mentioned “Samatha,” “Samapatti,” “Samapanna” and “Dhyana.” These four stages can be said to cover most people’s terms of expression [for meditation]. It also equates to the four meditation heavens of the Form Realm.
chapterbody 43: As to samadhi, it can be said to be the state of four concentrations on emptiness of the Formless Realm.
chapterbody 45: It is the perfect enlightenment.
chapterbody 46: It is the wonderful enlightenment.
chapterbody 47: This we must differentiate clearly and not be ambiguous.

chapterbody: once expounded on the Nine Stages of Kalachakra Meditation in Singapore’s International Expo Center. This event took up six exhibition halls and approximately sixty thousand people attended. This was unprecedented and is unlikely to be repeated.
chapterbody 3: My talk about each stage of meditation is as follows:
chapterbody 5: “Initial abiding” -- The mind has just slightly quieted down. The mind has many different thoughts. These thoughts are scattered and messy. Sitting time is short and the time that the mind stays quiet is also short. There is only about two minutes before distracting thoughts start to invade the mind. Even if one sits for a long time, the mind may only be settled for one or two minutes.
chapterbody 7: “Continued abiding” -- After having practiced meditation over a period of time, one is able to notice the distracted thoughts. In a back-and-forth exchange, once there is a distracting thought, one will rein the mind back in. The mind can be held steady for six minutes. In short, when one can bring the mind back and not have distracted thoughts for six minutes, one is considered to be in the “continued abiding” stage.
chapterbody 9: “Return abiding” -- In my opinion, the “continued abiding” stage will take a longer time, because this training of reining in the mind will take a long time to practice. When one enters the “return abiding,” stage, the mind can be held steady for as long as twelve minutes. After twelve minutes, once a distracted thought arises and is noticed, one’s mind can be immediately brought back. At this time, one can gradually control one’s mind.
chapterbody 11: “Near abiding” -- At every sitting, the mind is able to focus on one point for as long as twenty minutes. Within the twenty minutes, distracted thoughts cannot easily enter the mind. Even if they invade, they are quickly dismissed. There is an increase in concentration, which can be held for twenty minutes.
chapterbody 13: “Subdue abiding” -- The mind can concentrate for as long as thirty minutes. One is able to subdue one’s distracted thoughts. This is the “subdue abiding” stage. However, within these thirty minutes, one must be careful of the mind becoming relaxed. If it gets too relaxed, it can cause one to doze off. This kind of “drowsiness” could arise. This “sleepiness” is a type of error and one must increase one’s mental energy.
chapterbody 15: “Tranquil abiding” -- The state of “subdue abiding” is the key to entering samadhi, but it is easy to fall asleep; therefore, one needs to increase one’s mental alertness. When overcompensation of mental alertness occurs, one can instead get excited, which causes “arousal.” This “drowsiness” and “arousal” are both errors.
chapterbody 17: One is “falling asleep.”
chapterbody 18: Another is “mentally over-excited.”
chapterbody 19: The two need to be adjusted. Making it just right is most important. Time in this state is forty minutes.
chapterbody 21: “Most tranquil abiding” -- Time in this state can last up to one or two hours. Drowsiness or arousal becomes less and less. At this stage, the fruit of one’s labor is realized, and one can be considered a capable cultivator of meditation.
chapterbody 23: “Concentrated abiding” -- The time in meditation can be self- controlled and can be as long as one wants.
chapterbody 25: One can transform into Kalachakra or one’s principal deity. With little effort, one can settle into meditation.
chapterbody 27: There are no more occurrences of drowsiness or arousal.
chapterbody 28: (Like a person who sleeps well, they only need to lay down and they will be fast asleep. Effortlessly and mindlessly able to fall asleep and awaken. Completely self-controlled.)
chapterbody 30: “Equanimity abiding” -- no matters, no mind, no drowsiness, no arousal, no distractions, no thoughts.
chapterbody 32: No concept of time.
chapterbody 33: Witness the emptiness and vast ocean of bright light.
chapterbody 34: Entering of the deity into oneself and release of oneself into the cosmic consciousness.
chapterbody 35: Spiritual union with the principal deity. Spiritual union with deities.
chapterbody 39: I think:
chapterbody 40: The duration spent in meditation is only an estimate. Everyone is different.
chapterbody 41: First, overcome the distracted thoughts. Then, overcome drowsiness.
chapterbody 42: Thirdly, overcome arousal. Use “cessation.”
chapterbody 43: Use “observation.” Use “perseverance.”

chaptername: 007 The Analysis of the Seven-Point Meditation Posture
chapterbody: n typical meditation, the body is engaged in the seven-point meditation posture.
chapterbody 3: Let me elaborate the posture as follows:
chapterbody 5: 1. The rear portion of the seat is slightly higher. It should be raised by about two inches. If not, the body will lean backwards and be unable to sit straight.
chapterbody 7: 2. Sit cross-legged. It is also known as the full lotus position with the right leg pressing the left leg. Many people are unable to sit like this. Therefore, there is also the half lotus position, wish- fulfilling sitting position, and comfortable sitting position.
chapterbody 9: 3. Hands form the Meditation Mudra. With the palms facing up and resting on the lap, place the left hand over the right hand and have the tips of the thumbs touching. Some have the right hand over the left hand, which is also the Meditation Mudra. However, because left represents water and right represents fire, placing the left hand over the right hand has the meaning of calming afflictions.
chapterbody 11: 4. Shoulders are level.
chapterbody 15: 6. Two eyes aim at the nose.
chapterbody 17: 7. Press tongue against the upper palate.
chapterbody 19: I personally emphasize the relationship of the qi and channels. Therefore, I feel the important points are:
chapterbody 21: 1. Sit in full lotus position. This allows the Lower-body Qi [Apana] to enter the center channel. Proper sitting posture assists the warm sensation of inner fire to be initiated easier from the Dharma Producing Palace.
chapterbody 23: 2. Meditation Mudra. With the palms facing up and placed below the navel, it enables the Balancing Qi [Samana] to enter the center channel, also making it easier for the rising of the inner fire.
chapterbody 25: 3. Back straight with shoulders back. This enables the Circulating Qi [Vyana] to enter the center channel; thus, avoiding injury by qi or mistakes.
chapterbody 27: 4. Chin tucked in on the Adam’s apple. This enables the Upper- body Qi [Udana] to enter the center channel and easily calms the mind and ceases the distracting thoughts.
chapterbody 29: 5. Tongue pressed against the upper palate. This stabilizes the Heart Qi [Prana] in the center channel and enables clearer visualization.
chapterbody 31: I say:
chapterbody 32: Although most people are able to stack the legs in a full lotus position, still some are not. For those who cannot, personally, I don’t think they need to force it.
chapterbody 34: Half lotus position, wish-fulfilling sitting position, comfortable sitting position, and even sitting straight on the chair are all acceptable. In short, the backbone must be straight, the shoulders are level, Adam’s apple is pressed, the tongue is pressed against the upper palate, and the hands form the Meditation Mudra. Able to sit long is of the utmost importance.
chapterbody 36: However one sits, if one can press against the rectum with the left heel, press against the private part with the right heel or ankle, and draw and raise the lower qi as much as possible, one’s Lower-body Qi will be able to enter the center channel.
chapterbody 38: This is also the pith instruction.
chapterbody 40: As long as one sits correctly, jealousy, afflictions, anger, ignorance,
chapterbody 41: lust, arrogance, etc. will be eliminated.
chapterbody 43: I say:
chapterbody 45: The seven-point sitting posture is the beginning of meditation, which is also the beginning of the correct body posture, a still body, and body purification.
chapterbody 47: As for the Six-fire-pit Mudra Sitting Position, Celestial Sitting Posture, Crane-straddling Sitting Posture, Elephant Sitting Posture, and Lion Sitting Posture, these are sitting positions for special practices.

chapterbody 3: What is this “diao” [fall, drop]? What is this “ju” [lift, raise]?
chapterbody 4: Might as well use “stimulation” or “excitement”!
chapterbody 5: A mind that is too excited causes insomnia and likewise is unable to enter into meditation.
chapterbody 7: I remember there was a joke (attachment to excitement):
chapterbody 8: A patient told a psychiatrist, “All last night, I dreamt that I was playing baseball. I was quite bothered by this.”
chapterbody 9: The psychiatrist replied, “Ah! That is easy to treat. Which female star do you like most?”
chapterbody 10: The patient said, “Elizabeth Taylor!”
chapterbody 11: The psychiatrist said, “Then think of her before you go to sleep!”
chapterbody 12: The patient said, “That could never be, because I am up next to bat!”
chapterbody 16: The remedy for “arousal” (over excitement) is to relax. It is not easy to relax.
chapterbody 17: Therefore, use the:
chapterbody 18: “Skeleton Visualization”
chapterbody 19: “Breath Counting Exercise”
chapterbody 21: Let one’s excitement “settle down” first. It then becomes easy to enter into meditation.
chapterbody 25: In regarding to “distractedness”:
chapterbody 26: Many people say,
chapterbody 27: “As soon as I sit down, the mind is scattered with thoughts, which never stops. Thoughts about the past, the present, and the future keep coming up. They dash from the east to the west, to the north, and to the south. What can be done?”
chapterbody 28: I answered, “Use the method of cessation!”
chapterbody 29: He said, “Still unable to cease [thoughts]!”
chapterbody 30: I answered, “Recite the mantra ‘pei!’”
chapterbody 31: In this way, one thought gets one recitation of the mantra “pei”; keep on chanting as many “pei’s” as thoughts occur. Let all the restlessness and distractedness be “pei’d” away! Or, use the “visualization method” to stop these distracting thoughts.
chapterbody 35: Some people have told me, “As soon as I sit down to meditate, I start dozing off with sleep quickly following.” It is so true that I [the people] sleep many times a day!
chapterbody 36: This is “drowsiness.”
chapterbody 37: The remedy for “drowsiness” is to “tense up,” generally to invigorate the spirit.
chapterbody 38: Reciting the mantra “hum, hum!” can awaken oneself.
chapterbody 39: Or, one can get up and practice the Vajra Fist.
chapterbody 40: Use the right “wisdom.”
chapterbody 42: Recite the vajra mantras.
chapterbody 46: I say:
chapterbody 48: “Arousal” is caused by being too tense. Need to relax a bit. “Drowsiness” is caused by being lax. Need to tighten up a bit.
chapterbody 49: To be able to adjust the two to just right is most wonderful and makes it easier to enter into meditation.

chapterbody: y experience was:
chapterbody 3: The first time I sat down to meditate, my hands started to move by themselves. The movement was incredible!
chapterbody 4: The hands and feet moved as if in a dance.
chapterbody 5: The hands started waving.
chapterbody 6: The feet began stomping.
chapterbody 7: What’s even more amazing was that the body also started moving. The hands were forming mudras all by themselves. The legs were taking the seven-star steps.
chapterbody 8: I unexpectedly performed “spiritual movements.”
chapterbody 9: Because of this, I visited many well-known religious senior cultivators to consult them about this phenomenon.
chapterbody 10: One said, “Spiritual medium.”
chapterbody 11: Another said, “It is a god descending [onto the body].”
chapterbody 14: A fifth said, “Don’t know.”
chapterbody 15: The sixth said, “Movement of the spirit.”
chapterbody 16: A seventh said, “It is a possession by the devil.”
chapterbody 17: The eighth said, “It is the movement of qi.”
chapterbody 19: The Venerable Yin Shun was the one who said it was “the movement of qi.” Mr. Li Bingnan, a well-known lay Buddhist, was the one who said it was “possession by the devil.”
chapterbody 20: Afterwards, I started to research it myself. I discovered that I was in control of the movements. If I wanted it to move, then it would move. If I wanted it to stop, then it would stop.
chapterbody 21: I wrote a book called “The Study of Spirit Awakening”.
chapterbody 22: I believe it is the “movement of qi.” If the movement cannot be self- controlled, then the person is a spiritual medium.
chapterbody 23: If one is too fanatical about the awakening of the spirit or if one is being possessed by demonic spirits, then it will affect one’s livelihood and one will enter a demonic state.
chapterbody 25: My personal comprehension is:
chapterbody 26: Many religions or qigong masters use “spirit awakening” to lead
chapterbody 27: innocent people into believing in them (as a way of self-promotion).
chapterbody 28: “Spontaneous energy”, actually happens quite easily. It is one’s own
chapterbody 29: qi moving within the body. That’s all! Meditators are in control of it and pay no attention to it.
chapterbody 30: Furthermore, I want to tell everyone:
chapterbody 31: Practicing qi movement can strengthen the body. If practiced correctly, it can keep the body fit.
chapterbody 32: Practicing qi movement enables one to be close to supernatural beings and easily become a psychic.
chapterbody 33: Practice qi has its upsides.
chapterbody 34: Practice qi has its downsides.
chapterbody 39: I have also experienced:
chapterbody 40: During meditation, my whole body inexplicably started to itch here and there.
chapterbody 42: The soles were itching.
chapterbody 43: I was itching everywhere.
chapterbody 44: Once in meditation, the skin on my entire body seemed to become allergic. I didn’t know what to do.
chapterbody 45: However, as soon as I stood up and quit sitting, the itching immediately stopped.
chapterbody 46: In the beginning, I didn’t know why this was happening.
chapterbody 48: Someone said, “Impure body!”
chapterbody 49: Someone said, “Killed too many bugs!”
chapterbody 50: I say, “It is the movement of the qi on the skin! Let it be and it will
chapterbody 61: resolve itself!”

chapterbody: y experience was:
chapterbody 3: In a moment, I got the sensation of lightness as if the body had no weight and had acquired a pair of wings and, like the cloud, rose up into the empty space.
chapterbody 4: Like a bird, smoke, fog, and a UFO.
chapterbody 5: Wow! I found myself flying!
chapterbody 6: I entered into outer space.
chapterbody 7: It was the complete emptiness that made me feel so unreal. My fear inevitably surged. I quickly opened my eyes which returned everything to normal.
chapterbody 8: I closed my eyes again and the same thing happened. I was deeply afraid that I would enter the state of nothingness and be unable to return. What should I do?
chapterbody 9: Unable to return, death!
chapterbody 10: Isn’t this like “passing away while in a sitting posture”?
chapterbody 11: I requested guidance from senior cultivators:
chapterbody 14: “Illusion!”
chapterbody 15: “Out of body experience!”
chapterbody 16: “You should have taken this opportunity to go to Maitreya Buddha’s
chapterbody 17: palace in Tusita Heaven and listen to his Dharma discourse.”
chapterbody 18: Later on, I came to understand that the weightlessness was caused by the merging of qi with wind. With this knowledge, I was able to move on without fear!
chapterbody 22: As mentioned in one of my previous books I once became immovable like a mountain when engrossed in concentration during a meditation.
chapterbody 23: My body became a mountain.
chapterbody 24: The mountain peak extended into the clouds.
chapterbody 25: I was as heavy as a boulder.
chapterbody 26: I felt like a vajra, indestructible.
chapterbody 27: This kind of feeling was very stable and joyful, likeness to real meditation.
chapterbody 28: Two ghosts arrived.
chapterbody 29: One ghost said, “How come there is a huge mountain here that wasn’t here before? Let’s go around it.”
chapterbody 30: The other ghost said, “I have an axe. I will hack it and split it!”
chapterbody 31: The ghost raised the axe and gave it a whack.
chapterbody 32: Golden light shot out in four directions.
chapterbody 33: The axe broke!
chapterbody 35: However, the mountain was perfectly fine!
chapterbody 36: The two bitter ghosts angrily took the route to go around the mountain.
chapterbody 37: On the contrary, I was delighted.
chapterbody 39: Afterwards, I realized that it was qi merged with earth that transformed me to become heavy like a mountain.
chapterbody 40: One must continue the meditation.
chapterbody 44: A disciple once came to ask me:
chapterbody 45: “Every time I meditated, I would become a speck enveloped by darkness, where I would fall into an immeasurably deep, dark hole. In a flash of light, I would fall deeper and deeper as the sound of the wind echoed loudly in my ears.
chapterbody 46: Fear arose in my heart as I panicked at the thought of entering but being unable to escape. What shall I do?”
chapterbody 48: “As far as I know, all tactile sensations that happen during meditation are due to the merging of qi with either earth, water, fire, wind, space, or consciousness. Actually, there is no need to be afraid. However, if fear does not subside, then it is best to temporarily halt meditation to avoid side effects.”
chapterbody 49: All of this is worth studying.

chapterbody: y experience was:
chapterbody 3: Once, during meditation, my body felt cold from head to toe as if I was trapped in a world of ice and snow.
chapterbody 4: I got up to put on more clothes; I still felt cold. I put on a coat; I still felt cold.
chapterbody 5: Lastly, I wrapped myself in a comforter, but I still felt icy cold. Then, I realized that the coldness was from within and not from outside. Therefore, it didn’t matter what I put on.
chapterbody 6: I asked senior cultivators and their answers were of the following:
chapterbody 7: 1. “Bewitched”
chapterbody 8: 2. “Offended a spirit”
chapterbody 9: 3. “Weak body”
chapterbody 10: 4. “Lack of positive energy”
chapterbody 11: 5. “Kidney weakness”
chapterbody 13: Later on, I came to realize that this was qi merging with water. It happens to be that an abundance of water forms ice resulting in sensation of coldness.
chapterbody 15: On the other hand:
chapterbody 16: During another meditation session, just the opposite of being cold, I felt the sensation of heat. It was so hot that sweat came down like the rain.
chapterbody 17: My undergarments were all wet.
chapterbody 18: I took off all my clothing, but I was still feeling so hot that my head was letting off steam.
chapterbody 19: The heart was on fire.
chapterbody 20: This could also count as another kind of “inner fire”!
chapterbody 21: I felt, at the time, that if I was sitting in the middle of an ice cube, I could melt all the ice around me.
chapterbody 22: If a kettle with water was placed on my head, I could boil the water. The heat almost suffocated me to death.
chapterbody 23: Later on, I came to realize that this was the result of qi incorporating with fire.
chapterbody 25: Moreover:
chapterbody 26: The body may feel like tree bark, an astringent-like sensation.
chapterbody 27: The body may feel smooth like milk: very, very smooth, like it was smeared with a layer of oil, not slippery but smooth.
chapterbody 28: The body may feel very soft and flexible.
chapterbody 30: Or:
chapterbody 31: The private part may be as hard as a rock for a whole day. I read a segment in a sutra that states:
chapterbody 33: “Qi in sync with earth causes heaviness. Qi in sync with wind causes lightness. Qi in sync with water causes coldness. Qi in sync with fire causes heat. Qi in sync with earth causes astringency. Qi in sync with wind causes smoothness. Qi in sync with water causes flexibility. Qi in sync with fire causes rigidity.”
chapterbody 36: “Qi in sync with space causes the feeling of rising and falling. Qi in sync with consciousness produces many more spiritual transformations.”
chapterbody 37: Especially when in sync with consciousness:
chapterbody 39: become small,
chapterbody 40: travel spiritually,
chapterbody 42: hear far and near,
chapterbody 43: recall the cause and effect of three lifetimes,
chapterbody 44: transform into many bodies,
chapterbody 45: and many more.
chapterbody 47: To summarize, in the process of entering samadhi, cultivators will encounter many situations.
chapterbody 48: Based on my experience, these situations can be good or bad. To understand the underlying reason, one must find an experienced vajra master to point it out.

chapterbody: y experience is:
chapterbody 2: No movement of the body.
chapterbody 3: No movement of the mouth.
chapterbody 4: No movement of the mind.
chapterbody 5: No movement of the body is Seven-featured Vairocana Posture.
chapterbody 6: No movement of the mouth is not speaking.
chapterbody 7: No movement of the mind is void of all thoughts.
chapterbody 8: Another word:
chapterbody 9: No matters.
chapterbody 10: No mind.
chapterbody 11: These simple pith instructions seem easy, but are quite hard to follow, especially the non-emergence of thoughts and no mind.
chapterbody 14: In Vajrayana Buddhism, there is a phrase “sameness of mind and qi.” This phrase is the pith instruction.
chapterbody 15: Let me truthfully tell all of you:
chapterbody 16: The mind and body in every one of us is “moving.”
chapterbody 17: The eyes move -- to see.
chapterbody 18: The ears move -- to hear.
chapterbody 19: The nose moves -- to breath.
chapterbody 21: The body moves -- to act.
chapterbody 22: The mind moves -- to think.
chapterbody 23: Ha! Ha! There is not a thing that isn’t moving.
chapterbody 25: Furthermore:
chapterbody 26: One not only has things move externally, but also internally.
chapterbody 27: Brain -- functions.
chapterbody 28: Heart -- beats.
chapterbody 29: Arteries and veins -- circulates.
chapterbody 30: Lungs -- inflates and deflates.
chapterbody 31: Stomach -- digests.
chapterbody 32: Intestines -- transmits food through.
chapterbody 33: These are namely the movement of earth, water, fire, and wind.
chapterbody 35: Every cell in our body is:
chapterbody 36: Transforming
chapterbody 37: Transitioning
chapterbody 39: Breathing
chapterbody 40: Actually, the insides of a human body are all in a cycle of “executing, changing, transmitting,” which makes meditation difficult.
chapterbody 41: It is also difficult to quietly sit!
chapterbody 42: The most important pith instruction I would like to transmit here is “no sense of qi movement in the channels.”
chapterbody 44: That is:
chapterbody 45: When the qi is faint, the mind is faint, which leads to the thoughts being faint.
chapterbody 46: Maybe some people don’t understand the word “faint.”
chapterbody 48: Subtle. Extremely subtle.
chapterbody 49: This is the state of “primordial chaos.”
chapterbody 50: At this moment, the heavenly wind is silent, and the clouds disappear from the oceans and mountains.
chapterbody 51: In other words, when in this primordial chaotic state, there is nothing to be found. As a result, not a single thought arises.
chapterbody 52: Heaven descends to the earth. Sun and moon combine.
chapterbody 53: This is the cryptic meaning behind “samadhi.”
chapterbody 58: Only at this time can one truly reach the state of “no thought.” No self, no person, no sentient beings, no lifespan, this is when Buddha- nature reveals itself.

chapterbody 34: The mind easily runs away.

chapterbody 47: Six breaths = one fen (of water clock time).
chapterbody 48: Sixty fens = one ke (of water clock time).

chapterbody 58: We as mankind are deviants by karma.

chapterbody 62: Most importantly is we use the “breath counting method” to achieve “abiding qi.”
chapterbody 63: When one enters deep meditation, the Buddha-nature will be self revealed!

chapterbody: omeone asked of me, “What is true meditation?”

chapterbody 5: “Exactly.”
chapterbody 6: “Is there another way to explain ‘being one with the Tao’?”
chapterbody 7: “It is discovering one’s true mind and seeing one’s true nature. Discovering one’s true mind is to see clearly the ultimate truth. Seeing the true nature is to witness one’s Buddha nature.”
chapterbody 8: “How do you say it in even simpler language?”
chapterbody 9: “Natural pureness and perfect wisdom.”
chapterbody 10: “How does one cultivate this?”
chapterbody 11: “Abiding qi with a concentrated mind.”

chapterbody 17: Simplicity is no movement of thoughts.
chapterbody 18: One taste is unification to become one.
chapterbody 19: Non- cultivation is the Great Perfection.”

chapterbody: remember having seen a written account that went like this:
chapterbody 3: During the Ming Dynasty, there was a woodsman in the mountains chopping for wood. He chopped down a big tree that was as high as the sky.
chapterbody 4: The surprise was that within the center of the tree sat a cultivator in deep meditation. The woodsman realized that the cultivator was still alive, so he asked, “Sir, who are you?”
chapterbody 5: He answered, “Disciple of the Sixth Patriarch.”
chapterbody 6: The woodsman said, “The Sixth Patriarch was of the Tang Dynasty. We are now in the Ming Dynasty, which has passed through the Tang Dynasty, Song Dynasty, and Yuan Dynasty. This is really astonishing!”
chapterbody 7: He wanted to question some more but the cultivator already went back into deep meditation.
chapterbody 11: I myself think that:
chapterbody 12: The meditations similar to this belong to “empty mind meditation.”
chapterbody 13: This cultivator’s meditation lasted through four dynasties. It was amazing.
chapterbody 14: Recorded in the book by Senior Monk Hsu Yun: The senior monk was cooking taros.
chapterbody 15: Outside was snowing.
chapterbody 16: While the senior monk was sitting beside the fire stove, he accidently entered into deep meditation.
chapterbody 17: When he came out of the deep meditation, the snow had already stopped.
chapterbody 18: The hot coal in the fire stove had long been extinguished.
chapterbody 19: He took a look at the taros in the pot; it had mold already growing on it.
chapterbody 20: Once the Senior Monk Hsu Yun went into deep meditation, how much time passed by? This was marvelous!
chapterbody 21: This can also be an example of “empty mind meditation.”
chapterbody 25: Someone asked me, “Is empty mind meditation right or not?”
chapterbody 26: I answered, “I personally have had this experience. I once made an appointment to meet someone in two hours. I then wished to meditate awhile, so I closed the door to my room and drew the curtains to relax into meditation. I became completely oblivious. When I came out of meditation, two days and two nights had passed. But I felt like I had only sat for a little while!”
chapterbody 27: “These kinds of incidents have happened a lot of times. This state that is completely oblivious can last either a long or short time.”
chapterbody 28: I say:
chapterbody 29: Sitting quietly with the mind being empty (meditation) cannot be
chapterbody 30: categorized as right or wrong.
chapterbody 31: During the state of empty mind, one enters into a state of primordial chaos.
chapterbody 32: The universal dharma stream flows into one and one enters it as well.
chapterbody 33: One’s mind and body disappears.
chapterbody 34: One and void are merged together and there is no self, no person,
chapterbody 35: no sentient beings and no lifespan. There is not a thing.
chapterbody 36: One becomes like a “no mind cultivator.”
chapterbody 37: Under these conditions, the bright light emits from the ten directions, which can also imperceptibly save sentient beings.
chapterbody 39: Some of the cultivators of meditation oppose it.
chapterbody 40: Taoists believe that, in meditation, the fire needs to be ignited in the furnace to form a pellet of elixir, so they’re against “empty mind meditation.”
chapterbody 41: Some Vajrayana cultivators are also against it because, if all is empty, then it’s non-functional. It will not be useful for cultivators of the inner fire.

chaptername: 018 The Meditation of the Four Tantras
chapterbody: n Vajrayana Buddhism, the Four Tantras are:
chapterbody 2: Action Tantra
chapterbody 3: Performance Tantra
chapterbody 4: Yoga Tantra
chapterbody 5: Highest Yoga Tantra
chapterbody 6: I say:
chapterbody 7: Action Tantra is the level that most emphasizes the ritual procedure of Vajrayana Buddhism. It is comprised mostly of the prayer ritual.
chapterbody 8: Recitation ritual.
chapterbody 9: Dharma instrument ritual.
chapterbody 10: Invocation ritual.
chapterbody 11: Mandala offering ritual. Etc…
chapterbody 12: All are about the external appearance of Vajrayana and for attending upon [deities].
chapterbody 14: At the Performance Tantra, half of it then becomes meditation cultivation while the other half pertains to prayer rituals.
chapterbody 15: Actually, the meditation portion is just a small part of it.
chapterbody 16: Considerable importance is still placed on the rituals.
chapterbody 17: As we go further, into the Yoga Tantra, importance is placed on the internal meditation more so than the external practices of action yoga.
chapterbody 18: All practices are through the intrapersonal mind.
chapterbody 19: External action becomes less.
chapterbody 21: Action Tantra disappears.
chapterbody 22: Performance Tantra disappears.
chapterbody 23: Everything is on the meditation of the inner mind.
chapterbody 24: Highest Yoga Tantra is comprised solely of meditation of the inner mind.
chapterbody 25: Therefore, I say:
chapterbody 26: Action Tantra -- consists of no meditation.
chapterbody 27: Performance Tantra -- has some meditation.
chapterbody 28: Yoga Tantra -- consists of half meditation and half Action Tantra. Highest Yoga Tantra consists of all meditation.
chapterbody 32: When a cultivator reaches the Highest Yoga Tantra, it is the same as having all things created by the mind.
chapterbody 33: Food -- offerings.
chapterbody 34: Clothing -- boundary setting.
chapterbody 36: Action -- diligence.
chapterbody 37: Wealth -- giving.
chapterbody 39: Fame -- attainment.
chapterbody 40: Food -- offerings (for bardo deliverance).
chapterbody 42: Most people have never thought that “sexual desire” is actually meditation?
chapterbody 44: I say, “It’s no mistake!”
chapterbody 45: When yin merges with yang, it is:
chapterbody 46: Qi merges with qi.
chapterbody 48: Drops merge with drops.
chapterbody 49: Light shines on light.

chapterbody: hen the inner fire burns fiercely, it is the most suitable time to cultivate the non-leakage practice.
chapterbody 2: 1. Focus on the Mud Pellet Palace. Close your eyes and observe upward at the mud pellet. After awhile, the essence qi will rise and the yang will not leak [there will be no discharge].
chapterbody 3: 2. When yang rises and the discharge is about to happen, observe upward at the principal deity. Cool down and refresh the mind to firmly guard it. The essence will retract itself. However, one will have to wait awhile before the sensation of discharge subsides.

chapterbody 8: The fourth step of the cultivation method is:
chapterbody 9: Raising the inner fire up to the third eye chakra.
chapterbody 10: At the third eye chakra is the bodhicitta moon fluid (heavenly court water). The inner fire touches it and the moon fluid descends.
chapterbody 11: This is the phenomenon of the harmonization of fire and water.
chapterbody 12: Moon fluid descends from the third eye chakra to the throat chakra. (initial bliss)
chapterbody 13: Moon fluid descends from the throat chakra to the heart chakra. (supreme bliss)
chapterbody 14: Moon fluid descends from the heart chakra to the naval chakra. (most supreme bliss)
chapterbody 15: Moon fluid descends from the naval chakra to the sacral chakra. (innate bliss) This is the “descending.”
chapterbody 16: Then, there is the “ascending.”
chapterbody 17: Reversing to the “hold.”
chapterbody 18: Moon fluid ascends from the sacral chakra to the naval chakra. (initial level of emptiness)
chapterbody 19: Moon fluid ascends from the naval chakra to the heart chakra. (second level of emptiness)
chapterbody 20: Moon fluid ascends from the heart chakra to the throat chakra. (third level of emptiness)
chapterbody 21: Moon fluid ascends from the throat chakra to the third eye chakra. (the emptiness of emptiness)

chapterbody 36: The cultivation method for Taoism is:
chapterbody 37: Microcosmic circulation -- Every inhaled breath happens to end at the “baihui” point. Each exhaled breath enables the qi to go to the lower dantian.
chapterbody 38: The microcosmic circulation has to go through three passes, the Ren meridian, and the Du meridian.
chapterbody 39: Ren meridian goes upward from the perineum to the abdomen and chest, stopping at the eyes.
chapterbody 40: Du meridian goes upward from the perineum, following the spinal cord, passing baihui, stopping at the Gum Intersection.
chapterbody 42: Macrocosmic circulation-- Clear the meridians of Du, Ren, Chong, Dai, Yinjue, Yangjue, Yinwei, Yangwei.
chapterbody 43: I must remind you of several important points:

chapterbody: rom my own experiences, I can authenticate three words from cultivating meditation:
chapterbody 2: “Bliss” -- Dharma joy
chapterbody 3: “Brightness” -- Bright light
chapterbody 4: “Emptiness” -- Empty nature
chapterbody 5: I say:
chapterbody 6: When “qi” enters the central channel, the movement of qi in the central channel causes friction, which in turn generates “joy.”
chapterbody 7: When the “inner fire” moves through the central channel, or even through other channels within the body, it generates “joy.”
chapterbody 8: When the “inner fire” and the “moon fluid” move through the central channel, it causes friction and generates “bliss.”
chapterbody 9: This kind of “bliss” is indescribable. In one phrase:
chapterbody 10: “tingling with sensation and on cloud nine.”
chapterbody 11: Most people obtaining “dharma bliss” or “dharma joy” were unwilling to progress further, stopping at this stage. Actually, this is still only at the level of the “Desire Realm.”
chapterbody 15: I say:
chapterbody 16: The “inner fire” that rises is “warm,” “red,” and “illuminating.” This light can authenticate the word “brightness.”
chapterbody 17: The “inner fire” mixed with drops melted from the “moon fluid” is like oil entering fire, which creates a brilliant light. Due to this “merging of fire and water,” the central channel is unblocked, the five chakras are opened, and “brightness” is generated.
chapterbody 18: When the “merging of fire and water” clears all the channels in the body, even the pores will emit light.
chapterbody 19: The entire body illuminates light.
chapterbody 20: This stage is approximately near the “Form Realm.”
chapterbody 24: I say:
chapterbody 25: When we have cleared all the channels and opened the five chakras and the seven chakras, we have authenticated the Buddha-nature.
chapterbody 26: Authenticated:
chapterbody 27: “Boundless Emptiness”
chapterbody 28: “Boundless Consciousness”
chapterbody 29: “Nothing Whatsoever”
chapterbody 30: “Neither Thought Nor Absence of Thought”

chapterbody 34: “Nature of emptiness”
chapterbody 39: I say:
chapterbody 40: When “nature of emptiness” is revealed, one will realize that one and buddha are of one.
chapterbody 41: Even you, I, they, and all sentient beings, including everything in the universe, are of one. From this comes compassion.
chapterbody 42: Buddha
chapterbody 43: People
chapterbody 44: Everything
chapterbody 45: All are one. Because this is oneness with the “all dharmas (all phenomena) are empty of self-nature,” compassion and bodhicitta emerges.
chapterbody 46: Furthermore:
chapterbody 47: A cultivator will usually show compassion towards parents, children and friends. This is the average person’s perception.
chapterbody 48: But, once one’s cultivation has authenticated the “nature of emptiness,” in samadhi, owner and guest are one. The compassion emerged during this process is not limited to parents, children and friends. It can even be extended to non-karmic-related sentient beings.
chapterbody 49: (This is great compassion without karmic cause.)
chapterbody 50: What I have said here about “bliss,” “brightness” and “emptiness” are all from my own personal experiences. Maybe other people will have different thoughts on the matter.

chaptername: 031 Breathing Exercise Taught by Reverend Liaoming
chapterbody: aoist Qingzhen and Reverend Liaoming are the same person; thus, he is knowledgeable of both Taoism and Buddhism.
chapterbody 3: I had once asked him:
chapterbody 4: “How do we breathe during meditation?”
chapterbody 5: Reverend Liaoming said:
chapterbody 6: In Vajrayana Buddhism, there is:
chapterbody 7: Nine Cycle Breathing Exercise -- is complete breathing down to the lower dantian, “using the mind” to do nine segments of breathing.
chapterbody 8: Vajra recitation -- is to use a mantra syllable in complete breathing down to the lower dantian, holding the qi and then exhaling.
chapterbody 9: Treasure Vase Breathing Exercise -- utilizing complete breathing and the four breathing steps. Lastly, holding the breath and then forcefully expelling the breath.
chapterbody 10: (For details, my previous books have thoroughly described them.)
chapterbody 11: Reverend Liaoming said:
chapterbody 14: “The ingesting of qi”
chapterbody 15: “The refinement of qi”
chapterbody 16: It is a long story.
chapterbody 18: Zhuangzi said:
chapterbody 19: “Breathing is to expel stale air and inhale fresh air.”
chapterbody 21: “Inhale the air through the nose and hold it. Mentally count to one hundred twenty. Then, lightly exhale through the mouth. While inhaling and exhaling, one should never let one’s ears hear the sound of air in and out. Often inhale more than you exhale. Gradually practice holding the breath until one can mentally count up to a thousand.”
chapterbody 22: Tao Hongjing said:
chapterbody 23: “Circulation of qi is inhaled from the nose and expelled from the mouth. Lightly guiding the breath in and out. This is called long-drawn breaths.”
chapterbody 24: Sun Simiao said:
chapterbody 25: “If there is proper breathing of qi, then illness will not arise. If qi (breathing) is out of sync, then all illnesses will arise.”
chapterbody 26: A Taoist scripture also said:
chapterbody 27: “When one reaches embryonic breathing, that is, when one no longer breathes from the nose or the mouth, like a fetus in the womb, then one is not far from obtaining the Tao.”
chapterbody 29: My experience is:
chapterbody 30: 1. Complete breathing is very important, namely every inhaled breath needs to be long and deep into the lower dantian.
chapterbody 31: 2. Holding the breath at the lower dantian for a short time and gradually extend to a longer time.
chapterbody 32: 3. Not recommended to forcefully expelling the breath too often. About once a month is quite suitable.
chapterbody 33: 4. Slow, long, and refined breaths are the most suitable.
chapterbody 34: 5. Short, crude, and rushed breathing is rhythm breathing. It is suitable for when the “inner fire” is about to emerge.
chapterbody 36: 7. Exhaling more is not good.
chapterbody 37: 8. No breathing sounds can be heard.
chapterbody 38: 9. If one is unable to have a long inhalation and short exhalation, then at least make them the same length of time.
chapterbody 39: 10. Dissipating qi, best to use the mind. Let the qi disperse throughout the body and gradually dissipating.
chapterbody 40: 11. Don’t force oneself.
chapterbody 41: 12. Swallowing saliva at the time of descending qi.
chapterbody 42: 13. When exhaling, save a little in the mid-dantian. Don’t let it out completely.
chapterbody 43: 14. The time of day to practice breathing:
chapterbody 44: From Zi time at night to noon is vital qi.
chapterbody 45: From noon to Hai time at night is dead qi.
chapterbody 46: (I personally do not observe the time of day.)
chapterbody 47: 15. One has achieved the embryonic breathing method when one breathes through the pores.
chapterbody 48: 16. Achieving the embryonic breathing method nears obtaining the Tao.
chapterbody 49: 17. Practice the breathing exercises at a place where the air quality is good. At a place with polluted air, do not practice circulating the qi.
chapterbody 50: 18. A beginner of cultivation should not be hasty.
chapterbody 58: This is the principle behind achieving longevity.
chapterbody 61: 19. Advanced cultivators should try to combine the breathing with the entire body’s internal organs. All merged as one.
chapterbody 62: Every one of us has the primordial chaotic qi within us.

chapterbody: omeone asked, “Master Lu, how many different types of meditation are there?”
chapterbody 2: I answered, “Countless.”
chapterbody 3: “It sounds nonsensical. How do you explain it?”
chapterbody 4: “It’s not nonsensical. Concentrating is meditation.”
chapterbody 5: “Would you please give an example?”
chapterbody 6: “Like when a man and woman kiss, they are both absorbed in the moment, in a trance, the harmonious merging of two into one, feelings of happiness throughout the body. This is meditation. Meditation is to be entranced.”
chapterbody 7: “Kissing can get one into this state?”
chapterbody 8: “Of course.”
chapterbody 9: “Master Lu! I am asking about meditation in Buddhism.”
chapterbody 11: “There is conditioned meditation and unconditioned meditation.”
chapterbody 12: “What do you mean by conditioned meditation?”
chapterbody 13: “Written in a few chapters before, from the breath counting to discovering one’s true mind and witnessing the Buddha-nature, all is conditioned meditation.”
chapterbody 14: “What is unconditioned meditation?”
chapterbody 15: “Just sitting as usual with no attachments to anything, all is naturally running smoothly. That’s all.”
chapterbody 16: “How does this unconditioned meditation happen?”
chapterbody 17: “It is when there is no movement of the body, no movement of the mouth and no movement of the mind. It is when one is sitting in oblivion, entranced, with no thoughts. In my experience, at a moment when one’s mind is empty, the dharma stream of the universe instantly enters one’s body and one merges with the universe, becoming the emptiness of selflessness. All is then naturally attained.”
chapterbody 19: “Able to reach enlightenment?”
chapterbody 21: “Able to witness the Buddha-nature?”
chapterbody 22: “Able!”
chapterbody 23: “Then we should just cultivate unconditioned meditation. Why bother with the cultivation of conditioned meditation?”
chapterbody 24: “Cultivating unconditioned meditation is not easy.”
chapterbody 25: “Why is it not easy?”
chapterbody 26: “People have afflictions, knowledge hindrances, and all sorts of behavioral hindrances. It’s not easy to have “no thoughts.” (First practice conditioned meditation. Then, practice unconditioned meditation. Then, reverse to practicing conditioned meditation, alternating back and forth.)
chapterbody 27: “Then, should we start with cultivating conditioned meditation?”
chapterbody 28: “Expediency is the absolute.”
chapterbody 29: “Why do we have to cultivate meditation?”
chapterbody 30: I answered:
chapterbody 31: “Precepts, meditation, and wisdom are the Three Principles of Non-Outflow. To become a buddha, it takes both ‘meditation’ and ‘wisdom.’ They are the two wings to achieving Buddhahood.”

chapterbody: ccording to the Buddha’s discourse and the [Candrapradipasamadhi Sutra], besides being able to liberate oneself, awaken one’s mind to see the true Buddha-nature, and achieve Buddhahood in the present body, meditation also offers many other benefits.
chapterbody 3: 1. It improves one’s appearance to be more and more dignified. The appearance is affected by the mind. If the mind is at peace, then the appearance looks peaceful.
chapterbody 4: 2. It changes one’s behavior so as not to be extreme or resentful, and one therefore becomes naturally wise.
chapterbody 5: 3. It transforms one’s heart to be soft, compassionate, respectful, and humble.
chapterbody 6: 4. It allows one to recognize that the world is a big illusion, thus, breaking through all sufferings.
chapterbody 7: 5. It concentrates one’s mind so as not to be tempted by the outside world and wander.
chapterbody 8: 6. During a time of food shortage, one can subsist on ambrosia, dharma joy, qi, or meditative joy received through meditation.
chapterbody 9: 7. It gradually fades away all one’s worldly desires.
chapterbody 10: 8. It gradually relinquishes one of all strong attachments.
chapterbody 11: 9. Every second and minute of meditation results in virtuous fruition, not wasteful at all.
chapterbody 12: 10. If one’s meditation is firm, it will naturally provide a boundary protection that is unable to be penetrated by devious demons.
chapterbody 13: 11. As if one is in the buddha land, it enables one to obtain blessings from the guru, receive divine grace from one’s principal deity and be supported by one’s dharma protector.
chapterbody 14: 12. It gradually leads one to reach maturity on the path to liberation and enlightenment.
chapterbody 15: 13. Sufferings are removed, happiness is obtained.
chapterbody 16: 14. Gradually, the body produces light.
chapterbody 17: 15. Embraced by benevolent gods. Good fortune increases. Has the capability to do all significant benevolent deeds.
chapterbody 18: 16. Removes lifetimes of accumulated bad karma.
chapterbody 19: 17. Six Supernatural Powers are developed:
chapterbody 21: The psychic power of the heavenly ear
chapterbody 22: The ability to appear anywhere one wishes
chapterbody 23: The ability to read other people’s minds
chapterbody 24: The ability to perceive one’s own and others’ past lives Eradication of leakage
chapterbody 25: (Possessing dharma power and real attainment.)
chapterbody 26: 18. All the buddhas and bodhisattvas have revealed themselves in their entirety to bless one.
chapterbody 27: 19. All the dakinis have come to offer their assistance.
chapterbody 28: 20. Obtaining buddha’s wisdom.
chapterbody 29: 21. Has the ability to control one’s birth and death.
chapterbody 30: 22. Attain perfect Buddhahood.
chapterbody 32: This is my Zen poem:
chapterbody 33: Hundred thousand kalpas of lifetimes is nothing more than emptiness.
chapterbody 34: A short absence from the human world showed a difference.
chapterbody 35: In a state of meditation, people do not know.
chapterbody 36: Because of cause and effect, body is in a state of bliss.
chapterbody 39: In the mirror, capability and talent are superb.
chapterbody 40: In cultivating meditation, one’s personal deity naturally arrives.
chapterbody 41: When the two bodies are in yogic union.
chapterbody 42: The illusory body thereby enters the palace of heaven.
chapterbody 44: And:
chapterbody 45: Dharma body originally abides in the Tathagata realm.
