chapterbody: omeone asked: “Should everyone cultivate meditation?”
chapterbody 2: I answered, “Yes, but there are many ways. One needs to choose a method.”
chapterbody 3: “How so?”
chapterbody 4: “It depends on the individual. Before meditation, there are many preliminary practices that must be completed first.”
chapterbody 5: I say:
chapterbody 6: Those who are arrogant need to practice full prostration.
chapterbody 7: Those who are stingy need to cultivate the Great Mandala Offerings.
chapterbody 8: Those with strong desires need to cultivate the Skeleton Visualization Method.
chapterbody 9: Those who are hateful need to cultivate the bodhicitta of placing oneself in someone else’s shoes.
chapterbody 10: Those who are obsessed with fame and status need to cultivate the Illusive Practice.
chapterbody 11: That is why, it is best to first make the appropriate adjustments based on one’s temperament prior to starting meditation.
chapterbody 15: Someone else asked, “To cultivate meditation, one needs to have a long-term commitment. Nowadays, people do not have the determination. What can one do?”
chapterbody 16: I say, “Perseverance is brought about through training!”
chapterbody 17: For example:
chapterbody 18: My writing.
chapterbody 19: My cultivation.
chapterbody 21: By having studied in a military school, I had to follow the prescribed order to exercise my physique. Consequently, it disciplined my perseverance.
chapterbody 22: Someone asked,
chapterbody 23: “Can we as lay Buddhists go dancing, sing karaoke, attend parties, play mahjong, drink some alcoholic beverages, or visit casinos?”
chapterbody 24: I answered:
chapterbody 25: “All physical activities that can confuse one’s mentality or cause addiction so that one is unable to extricate oneself are not good.
chapterbody 26: When it is unavoidable, then one is allowed to engage in these activities. However, the engagement should be limited. It is not good if one is often doing these activities.
chapterbody 27: In short, one must have control. At the end of the enjoyment, one must retrieve the playful heart and get back on track with cultivation.”
chapterbody 29: Someone asked,
chapterbody 30: “How do we live an orderly life?”
chapterbody 31: I answered:
chapterbody 32: “Follow the Eightfold Path. The Eightfold Path will keep one away from a disorderly life.
chapterbody 33: Right View
chapterbody 34: Right Contemplation
chapterbody 36: Right Action
chapterbody 37: Right Livelihood
chapterbody 39: Right Mindfulness
chapterbody 40: Right Concentration
chapterbody 42: Someone asked,
chapterbody 43: “What if karmic obstacles appear?”
chapterbody 44: I answered,
chapterbody 45: “One should consult a seasoned cultivator or an experienced meditation cultivator.
chapterbody 46: One can also consult one’s root guru and should be appropriately respectful to the root guru.
chapterbody 47: Avoid the case of the blind leading the blind.”

chaptername: 035 How to Have the Capability to Differentiate
chapterbody: omeone asked, “Aside from asking a spiritually wise guide or one’s root guru, how do we ourselves develop the capability to differentiate?”
chapterbody 2: I answered:
chapterbody 3: “The capability to differentiate comes from experiences. For example, by reading a lot of Master Lu’s books, one will gradually become knowledgeable, thus developing the capability to differentiate.”
chapterbody 4: “The point is what are we differentiating?”
chapterbody 5: “Time, place, timing, situation.”
chapterbody 6: “Could Master Lu please explain in detail.”
chapterbody 7: My answer:
chapterbody 8: Time — The time one is engaged in samadhi. The length of time of concentration and the sensations experienced during meditation.
chapterbody 9: Place — Choose a cave that harbored a greatly accomplished practitioner, a place that has blessing power. Or, one can find a spiritually efficacious spot using feng shui and geomancy.
chapterbody 10: Timing — One must know the best time for meditation cultivation, what level of the inner fire should be exercised, when is best to use the “gentle fire,” and when is best to use the “fierce fire.”
chapterbody 11: Situation — The situations that arise during meditation are as numerous as the number of hairs on a cow. One must know how to treat the various situations. For example, how to remedy the eight tactile sensations, the various kinds of negligence and troubles, etc.
chapterbody 13: Someone asked, “In what way is one considered to be in the unexcelled state of Perfect Enlightenment?”
chapterbody 14: I answered:
chapterbody 15: “This also needs the ability to differentiate. One must know what is the sensation of bliss, what is brightness, and what is emptiness. Be careful not to be complacent. Obtaining bliss is not absolute, and neither is obtaining the brightness nor the emptiness. Obtaining the unexcelled state of Perfect Enlightenment is however considered absolute. Perfect attainment is not bliss, brightness, or emptiness. Do not be complacent. Gradual improvement is not the unexcelled state of Perfect Enlightenment.”
chapterbody 16: Someone asked,
chapterbody 17: “Then, what is the unexcelled state of Perfect Enlightenment?”
chapterbody 18: I answered:
chapterbody 19: “Liberation
chapterbody 21: Awaken the mind
chapterbody 22: Realize the Buddha-nature
chapterbody 23: Still quiescence
chapterbody 24: Nirvana”
chapterbody 26: “Who can authenticate?”
chapterbody 27: “Only a buddha knows a buddha!”
chapterbody 28: “Are there any scriptures that can validate this?”
chapterbody 29: I answered:
chapterbody 30: “It is validated in the [Diamond Sutra] and the [Lankavatara Sutra].”
chapterbody 31: My suggestion:
chapterbody 32: 1. A cultivator of meditation must have guidance from a master. This master must be enlightened and can see the Buddhanature. Differentiate carefully.
chapterbody 33: 2. The cultivator has to be able to give up worldly attachments.
chapterbody 34: Has to have the willpower and perseverance to yearn for samadhi.
chapterbody 35: 3. Has no worries and is not irritable; diligently striving for the love of dharma.
chapterbody 36: 4. Has wisdom.
chapterbody 37: 5. Capable of differentiating.
chapterbody 38: 6. Recognizes the sequential stages of cultivation. Moreover, of course, one must differentiate. Do not deviate from it.

chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “What are the main mistakes and deviations that arise during meditation? Master Lu, please instruct us.”
chapterbody 3: I answered, “There are actually many mistakes and deviations. I will point out some common ones.”
chapterbody 4: “What are the most common ones?”
chapterbody 5: I laughed out loud and said, “Number one is practicing in a joking manner.”
chapterbody 6: Someone asked,
chapterbody 7: “What do you mean by that?”
chapterbody 8: “It’s like fishing for one day followed by drying the net in the sun for three days. Having heard how good meditation is, following suit, one sits for meditation. Meditate for awhile, then rest several times. Not following the cultivation sequence, one just sits with the thoughts running wild.”
chapterbody 9: “In what way is considered the real cultivation of meditation?”
chapterbody 10: “Firstly, do not slack in practice.”
chapterbody 11: “What is the second?”
chapterbody 12: “Many people wish to practice meditation, but do not know how to start. After listening to Guru Master’s dharma discourse, one cannot remember what was heard every time. After reading Master Lu’s books, one simply can never grasp the main points. One wishes to cultivate meditation, but never knows where to begin.”
chapterbody 14: “Finish reading this book, [Pith Teachings to Samadhi]. Focusing on the main points is a start to meditation. This is the second important point.”
chapterbody 15: “What is the third mistake?”
chapterbody 16: “Falling asleep as soon as one sits down to meditate. Haha! This is drowsiness. Sleeping is not meditation!”
chapterbody 17: “What can be done?”
chapterbody 18: “To not fall asleep, one must arouse the mind, be more alert and advance in the practices according to the sequence of stages. In Vajrayana, one first cultivates the qi, then inner fire and light drops, followed by the opening of the channels and five chakras.”
chapterbody 20: “I know the fourth mistake! It must be “arousal” caused by an overly excited mind! Right?”
chapterbody 21: “An excited mind can cause thoughts to run wild. This is a major hindrance to entering into samadhi. Relax a little and keep calm. No matter what method of the meditation applied, one should always relax and keep calm. This is the correct way.”
chapterbody 22: “Any other mistakes?”
chapterbody 23: “If one falls asleep or becomes too excited then goes along with it without taking any action to stop it, this is failure to respond in the moment. Letting sleep or excitement take over is also a mistake.”
chapterbody 24: “What else?”
chapterbody 25: “In summary, one must have faith in the cultivation of meditation. One must have enough diligence, be able to implement one’s perseverance. One does not necessarily have to use ascetic practices, such as the ancient ways of depriving oneself of food, hanging upside down, copying animals, submerging oneself in water, subjecting oneself to needle piercings, etc. Do not be too extreme. In addition, always remember the root guru’s teachings. When one doesn’t understand something, one needs to quickly seek answers. Do not fall into the devil’s trap.”

chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “My mind is very distracted. Every time during meditation, distracting thoughts run non-stop. Master Lu, how can you help me?”
chapterbody 3: I repeated the words of the Bodhidharma,
chapterbody 4: “Give me your mind!”
chapterbody 5: “I can’t find my mind.”
chapterbody 6: “I have already calmed your mind.”
chapterbody 7: (This is an imitation of Bodhidharma. A mind not able to be found is the same as not having a mind. Without a mind, then it can’t be distracted.)
chapterbody 8: Someone asked, “What can be done when the mind is distracted?”
chapterbody 9: I answered, “Stop.”
chapterbody 10: “What if it can’t be stopped!”
chapterbody 11: “Use visualization.”
chapterbody 13: “Cease it with visualization and it will stop!”
chapterbody 15: Also:
chapterbody 16: Someone asked, “Why does the mind get distracted?”
chapterbody 17: I answered:
chapterbody 18: “When one looks at what one is not supposed to be looking at, listening to what one is not supposed to be listening to, this will cause a distracted mind.”
chapterbody 19: “What can we do about this?”
chapterbody 20: “Don’t look. Don’t listen. Don’t speak. These “three monkeys” are very important. As long as one doesn’t look, doesn’t listen, and doesn’t speak, a chaotic mind will become calm. People often say, out of sight, out of mind (pure)!”
chapterbody 21: “Are there any other reasons?”
chapterbody 22: “Too many worldly affinities, too many outside temptations, too many mundane tasks, too many social engagements. These all cause the mind to be distracted. Disturbances come one after another. How can it not be distracted? The mind jumps from one disturbance to another. Could the mind not be distracted?”
chapterbody 24: “How can we avoid these worldly affinities, temptations, mundane tasks, and engagements?”
chapterbody 25: “Minimize them as much as possible. Eliminate the ones that are not necessary. Going into a retreat is the best option, if there is an opportunity. If one can go into retreat, the mind will naturally calm down!”
chapterbody 26: “If in retreat, the mind is still distracted, what can one do?”
chapterbody 27: “It is then solely from the cultivator’s own mind. Recalling old memories that linger in the mind. The only method is to discard them like trash. Totally forget them.”
chapterbody 28: “What can be done if these memories cannot be trashed?”
chapterbody 29: “Use visualization to stop them. For example, visualizing impermanence, visualizing impurity, this is the method of ‘throwing out the trash’!”
chapterbody 30: “If one is still not able to throw them away, then what?”
chapterbody 31: “Then go ‘Jia Sai’!” (Translator’s note: Taiwanese for ‘eat poop’)
chapterbody 32: “Master Lu! How can you say that?”
chapterbody 33: I answered,
chapterbody 34: “Rotten wood cannot be carved!”

chaptername: 038 How to Distinguish the Four Levels of Meditation
chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “How do you distinguish among the four levels of meditation?”
chapterbody 4: I answered, “
chapterbody 5: The difference is very minute. Sometimes it even overlaps. In fact, the difference is barely distinguishable.”
chapterbody 6: “Can you give more details?”
chapterbody 7: “Yes.”
chapterbody 8: I said:
chapterbody 9: I am accustomed to distinguishing the four levels of meditation according to the four realms of heaven. For example:
chapterbody 10: The Desire Realm of Heaven —
chapterbody 11: A little bit of enlightenment (slightly enlightened)
chapterbody 14: The appearance of dharma happiness
chapterbody 15: Has lightly touched upon meditation
chapterbody 16: (This is the initial level of meditation.)
chapterbody 18: Next:
chapterbody 19: Has pure trust in meditation (pure faith)
chapterbody 21: Has dharma happiness (bliss)
chapterbody 22: Able to enter deeper into meditation
chapterbody 23: (This is the second level of meditation.)
chapterbody 24: The Heaven of Form Realm —
chapterbody 25: Has realized the intrinsic equality of all beings. (Fair and Impartial Wisdom of the Nature)
chapterbody 26: Has only a single thought
chapterbody 27: Development of wisdom (prajna)
chapterbody 28: Brightness appears
chapterbody 29: Able to enter the most peaceful, deep meditation
chapterbody 30: (This is the third level of meditation.)
chapterbody 31: The Formless Realm of Heaven —
chapterbody 32: No sufferings, n
chapterbody 33: Everything is equally the same
chapterbody 34: Emptiness
chapterbody 36: Neither thought nor absence of thought
chapterbody 37: (This is the fourth level of meditation.)
chapterbody 39: Someone asked,
chapterbody 40: “Do these four levels of meditation only occur in Buddhism?”
chapterbody 42: “No, it can apply to any cultivators of any religion who cultivate meditation. That is, cultivators of meditation of all religious will all experience the same.”
chapterbody 43: “What is buddha’s highest level of meditation?”
chapterbody 44: “Absolute universal enlightenment, wonderful enlightenment, attaining the unexcelled state of Perfect Enlightenment.”
chapterbody 45: “Are there any other unique feelings in the fourth level of meditation?”
chapterbody 46: I answered:
chapterbody 48: Free from afflictions
chapterbody 49: Happy
chapterbody 50: Unhindered
chapterbody 52: Conducts no evil practices and no wicked deeds.
chapterbody 61: Demonic thoughts do not easily arise
chapterbody 63: The stream of primordial qi (dharma stream) fills the entire body, flowing to every part of the body. In other words, the body is first entirely filled with qi (genuine qi) as a sign of the merging of heaven and human. Then, there is the gradual emergence of light until one’s entire body emits light. Finally, there is absolute enlightenment and one enters into the still quiescence.

chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “Master Lu! This book has been emphasizing meditation. If a cultivator sits for meditation once a day, four times a day, or six times a day, would you say they have deviated from the Buddhadharma?”
chapterbody 3: I answered, “I follow the middle way.”
chapterbody 4: “Nowadays, many Buddhist cultivators practice differently from Master Lu. Some of them emphasize conducting good deeds first. What is your opinion of this?”
chapterbody 5: “The Buddhism in China advocates the teachings of Mahayana. Most cultivators like to think of themselves as bodhisattvas promoting good deeds. It is understandable. However, by placing too much focus on doing good deeds and neglecting to raise one’s level of spirituality by not cultivating meditation, one is still not able to be liberated in the end.”
chapterbody 6: “Master Lu, what do you make of this?”
chapterbody 7: “Advocating Humanistic Buddhism and promoting ‘pureland in the human world’ are both catchy propositions. But doesn’t it diminish one’s mind to renunciate? Lacking and not promoting the mind to renunciate is of course straying away from the original intent of Buddha’s doctrines. These are my thoughts.”
chapterbody 8: “What do you think about some of the great masters establishing the biggest Buddhist temples or becoming the biggest mountain tops [Editor’s note: metaphor for becoming the biggest Buddhist organizations]?”
chapterbody 9: “Building onto the mountain tops or establishing the largest temples are all okay. But compared to the Buddha’s time of living in caves, under the trees, or in the burial grounds, is quite a big difference. Therefore, my suggestion is to find a balance. Build the temples but also must cultivate meditation.”
chapterbody 10: “Mahayana cultivators criticize Hinayana cultivators as selfish and ignorant. What do you think?”
chapterbody 11: “They should not. The Four Noble Truths is the dharma spoken by the Buddha. Hinayana cultivators follow the precepts. They emphasize renunciation. That is the cornerstone of Buddhism that should not be overlooked. Hinayana cultivators place importance on cultivation of the self for renunciation. If one doesn’t cultivate on the self, then how can one attain liberation from the cycle of reincarnation in the end.”
chapterbody 12: “There are some Buddhists who say that Hinayana Buddhism was not taught by the Buddha. Is this true?”
chapterbody 13: “When the Buddha initially turned the dharma wheel, it was at Sarnath Park, not far from Varanasi. What was taught was sufferings, origins of suffering, cessation, and the path. Hinayana dharma placed importance on cultivation of the self. This is the original stepping stone for Buddhism. One should not criticize or slander it.”
chapterbody 15: “Modern day Buddhism does not place importance on precepts. What do you think of this?”
chapterbody 16: “That is not right.”
chapterbody 17: “Mahayana Buddhists save sentient beings by promoting conducting good deeds. Would that be right?”
chapterbody 18: “Yes, but one must also cultivate the self. Cultivation of the self and
chapterbody 19: deliverance of sentient beings must be done simultaneously!”
chapterbody 20: “Do you like Hinayana Buddhism or Mahayana Buddhism?”
chapterbody 21: “Whether Hinayana, Mahayana, or Vajrayana, these three are all important. If there is no Hinayana then there will be no precepts and meditation. How will we be able to extricate from the cycle of reincarnation?”
chapterbody 22: “Why are there deviations from the Buddhadharma?”
chapterbody 23: “When the great masters do not clearly clarify Buddha’s doctrine but only suit their speech to the audience, deviations occur naturally!”

chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “Master Lu! In your opinion, is it necessary to cultivate all the meditation approaches?”
chapterbody 3: I answered,
chapterbody 4: “It is not necessary. Cultivate according to each individual’s idiosyncrasies.”
chapterbody 5: People asked, “What do you mean?”
chapterbody 6: I answered:
chapterbody 7: Let me use the simplest analyses as follows:
chapterbody 8: Method of breath counting -- is for the cessation of scattered thoughts.
chapterbody 9: Contemplation on impurity -- is for the cessation of greed.
chapterbody 10: Contemplation on compassion -- is for the cessation of anger and hate.
chapterbody 11: Contemplation on causes and conditions -- is for the cessation of ignorance.
chapterbody 12: (Every cultivator must understand their own idiosyncrasies and cultivate accordingly, which will naturally lead to fruition.)
chapterbody 14: An impatient temperament needs to cultivate “breath counting.”
chapterbody 15: A slow temperament needs to cultivate “impurity.”
chapterbody 16: Someone with high wisdom needs to cultivate “compassion.”
chapterbody 17: Someone with great compassion needs to cultivate “causes and conditions.”
chapterbody 21: Someone asked,
chapterbody 22: “How is cultivating these approaches beneficial?”
chapterbody 23: I answered:
chapterbody 24: The method of using breath counting can “calm and abide the mind.”
chapterbody 25: Contemplation on compassion can increase one’s good fortune and virtues.
chapterbody 26: Contemplation on impurity can increase the will to renunciate from worldliness.
chapterbody 27: Contemplation on causes and conditions can help one understand emptiness.
chapterbody 28: (I personally feel without the mind to renunciate from this world, then cultivation will not be effective. The best result from cultivation is to become a buddha, a mediocre result from cultivation is to become bodhisattva or an arhat, and the lowest result from cultivation is to become a heavenly being. All of these will not be possible without the mind to renunciate from this world.)
chapterbody 29: I don’t particularly like:
chapterbody 30: Humanistic Buddhism
chapterbody 31: Pureland in the human world
chapterbody 32: Heaven in the human world
chapterbody 33: (Because this is the idea of utopia, or Great Unity. It is impossible in reality.)
chapterbody 34: (Editor’s note: Great Unity or “Datong” is a neo-Confucian philosophy of a great community.)
chapterbody 36: The contemplation on impurity stresses importance on:
chapterbody 37: Death is a common factor, no one can avoid it.
chapterbody 39: Think of bruises from hemorrhaging.
chapterbody 40: Think of the body infested by insects.
chapterbody 41: Think of the body going bad.
chapterbody 42: Think of the body producing puss.
chapterbody 43: Think of body decaying.
chapterbody 44: Think of the bones and ashes of the dead.
chapterbody 45: This is the visualization of death. It will help one to give up everything such as health, youth, beauty, body figure, sexual desires, greed, and various other strong attachments.
chapterbody 46: Think about it:
chapterbody 47: In the past, there have been many saints, buddhas, sages, emperors, magnates, etc., not one could avoid death.
chapterbody 48: Nowadays, there are many neighbors, parents, children; no matter if they are elders, middle-aged, adolescents, or youths, all are walking towards death.
chapterbody 49: No one can avoid death.
chapterbody 50: The future will become the present and the present will become the past.
chapterbody 51: Therefore, why are you still having such strong attachment to greed, hatred, ignorance, doubt, and arrogance.
chapterbody 52: Then, I would no longer have strong attachments to money, beauty, status, food, and etc!
chapterbody 61: Death is unavoidable.
chapterbody 62: I personally often contemplate on impurity.

chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “Master Lu often brings up the topic of enlightenment. Meditation can lead to seeing the Way. Seeing the Way is enlightenment. Then, can you please tell us how to reach enlightenment?”
chapterbody 3: I answered:
chapterbody 4: Cultivation in Buddhism has five paths:
chapterbody 5: Path of Accumulation
chapterbody 6: Path of Application
chapterbody 7: Path of Seeing
chapterbody 8: Path of Cultivation
chapterbody 9: Path of Absolute
chapterbody 10: I personally feel that to see the path of enlightenment requires both wisdom and meditation.
chapterbody 11: I say,
chapterbody 12: “Among Buddhist scriptures, many belong to enlightening sutras, such as the Prajnaparamita Sutra, in which the profound meaning of enlightenment is hidden. If one can understand this sutra, then one has reached enlightenment. Also, if, during meditation, one reaches the Formless Realm of the fourth level of meditation, one is approaching ‘Seeing the Way.’ By also having confirmation from one’s guru, it will naturally lead to fruition.”
chapterbody 14: “What are the benefits of enlightenment?”
chapterbody 15: I answered,
chapterbody 16: “Breaking away from wrong views.”
chapterbody 18: “What are wrong views?”
chapterbody 19: “In Buddhism, there are a lot of terminology that most people do not understand, such as: the view of attachment to views, the view of attachment to precepts, the view of self, wrong views, views of attachment to extremes. These terms are quite bothersome.”
chapterbody 21: The wrong views I am talking about are more direct:
chapterbody 22: 1. Attachment to I or mine
chapterbody 23: 2. Preconceptions/biases
chapterbody 24: 3. No cause and effect or reincarnation
chapterbody 25: 4. Stubborn misinterpretation
chapterbody 26: 5. Views that are misled
chapterbody 27: And so on and so forth. Only an enlightened practitioner can break through the obstacle of afflictions and obstacle of what is known.
chapterbody 29: I say:
chapterbody 30: Path of Accumulation cannot break through these obstacles.
chapterbody 31: Path of Application cannot break through these obstacles.
chapterbody 32: Only the Path of Seeing (enlightenment) can one break through.
chapterbody 33: Therefore, we can say:
chapterbody 34: Enlightened! Anger has all vanished!
chapterbody 36: Enlightened! No longer stubborn!
chapterbody 37: Enlightened! Attachments have disappeared!
chapterbody 39: Enlightened! Unhindered.
chapterbody 40: Enlightened! Omnipresent.
chapterbody 42: My instruction for everyone is to thoroughly comprehend the three dharma seals! Because the three dharma seals are the truth.
chapterbody 43: “Impermanence”
chapterbody 44: “No self”
chapterbody 45: “Emptiness”
chapterbody 46: Someone asked, “Besides breaking away from wrong views, what other benefits does enlightenment have?”
chapterbody 48: Even more steadfast in faith.
chapterbody 49: Even more diligent in cultivation.
chapterbody 50: Upholding precepts.
chapterbody 61: The mind to renunciate becomes more genuine.

chaptername: 042 Ten Types of Mindfulness that Aid in Meditation
chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “We aspire for deep meditation. How can we enhance our everyday meditation practice?”
chapterbody 3: I said,
chapterbody 4: “The ten types of mindfulness!”
chapterbody 5: Someone asked,
chapterbody 6: “Which ten types of mindfulness?”
chapterbody 7: I answered:
chapterbody 8: Mindful of the buddha -- When contemplating the buddha, visualize buddha’s elegance to purify oneself.
chapterbody 9: Mindful of the dharma -- When contemplating the dharma, comprehend the wisdom of the buddha to obtain the buddha wisdom.
chapterbody 10: Mindful of the sangha -- When contemplating on the sangha, understand the precepts of the sangha to keep one’s conduct from deviating.
chapterbody 11: Mindful of the precepts -- When contemplating on the precepts, uphold all precepts so that one’s mind is calm and immovable.
chapterbody 12: Mindful of giving -- When contemplating on giving, one’s deed must be beneficial to both oneself and others at the same time, increasing one’s merits.
chapterbody 13: Mindful of heaven -- When contemplating on heaven, one obtains heaven’s support and protection from possession by demons.
chapterbody 14: Mindful of death -- When contemplating on death, visualize everything is emptiness to terminate greed.
chapterbody 15: Mindful of the body -- When contemplating on the body, understand the frailness of the body to remove greed, anger, and ignorance.
chapterbody 16: Mindful of the breath -- When contemplating on the breath, understand that if a breath does not come, then everything is gone.
chapterbody 17: Mindful of tranquility -- When contemplating on tranquility, understand the original states of life, instantly enlightened.
chapterbody 19: Someone asked,
chapterbody 20: “What does being mindful of heaven have anything to do with the Buddhadharma?”
chapterbody 21: I answered,
chapterbody 22: “The heavens guard the Buddhadharma, as well as protect practitioners of the Buddhadharma. How can we not respect the heavens?”
chapterbody 23: Someone asked,
chapterbody 24: “How are these ten types of mindfulness different from the four foundations of mindfulness?”
chapterbody 25: I answered:
chapterbody 26: The four foundations of mindfulness are:
chapterbody 27: Contemplating on the impurity of the body is equivalent to mindfulness of the body. Purify oneself by contemplating the impurity of one’s body.
chapterbody 28: Contemplating on the sufferings resulting from emotions is equivalent to the mindfulness of tranquility. It will strengthen our mind to renunciate.
chapterbody 29: Contemplating impermanence of the mind is equivalent to mindfulness of the precepts. It will strengthen our immovable mind.
chapterbody 30: Contemplating “no-self” is equivalent to mindfulness of death, no “I” or “mine.” Enlightenment follows.
chapterbody 31: Someone asked,
chapterbody 32: “Does the four foundations of mindfulness have any other meanings?”
chapterbody 33: I answered:
chapterbody 34: Contemplate on the impurity of the body -- emptiness of self
chapterbody 35: Contemplate on the suffering from emotions -- emptiness of phenomena
chapterbody 36: Contemplate on the impermanence of the mind -- emptiness of the mind
chapterbody 37: Contemplate on no-self -- emptiness of nature
chapterbody 39: I added:
chapterbody 40: Manjushri Bodhisattva said:
chapterbody 42: Emptiness of the mind
chapterbody 43: Emptiness of dharma
chapterbody 44: Emptiness of nature
chapterbody 45: Someone asked,
chapterbody 46: “Why do we have to learn this?”
chapterbody 48: “Purification!”
chapterbody 49: “In the cultivation of meditation, why do you say we should start with counting the breath?”
chapterbody 50: “The counting of the breath is the foundation. For cultivating the qi, we begin with the counting of the breath. Pay attention to the inhalations, exhalations and the length of breath, filling the entire body. The breathing method is for concentration.”
chapterbody 61: From the breathing, it produces:

chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “In December 2015 in Hong Kong, Grand Master Lu held a Grand Ceremony of the White Lotus King, the second king of Shambhala. The splendor of the occasion was unprecedented. White Lotus King wrote the [Immaculate Light Treatise]. What are its main points?”
chapterbody 3: I answered,
chapterbody 4: “Emptiness.”
chapterbody 5: Someone asked,
chapterbody 6: “What kind of emptiness is it?”
chapterbody 7: “Immaculate.”
chapterbody 8: “In what way is it immaculate?”
chapterbody 9: “This is the original meaning of the Kalachakra Dharma. It indicates that as the wheel of time rolls past, it obliterates everything - mountains, rivers, the earth, and people. All is destroyed to nothingness; everything becomes nothing. This is emptiness.”
chapterbody 10: “The world is contaminated, i.e. people have karma. How can it be empty? How can it be immaculate?”
chapterbody 11: “What’s past is already past. What’s present will also become the past. The future will become the present and that eventually will be the past too. This is using time to obliterate everything; thus, emptiness.”
chapterbody 14: “The rock is hard. How can it be empty?”
chapterbody 15: “No self-nature; thus, emptiness.” (Rocks can be worn away by the wind.)
chapterbody 16: “Is the sky empty in nature?”
chapterbody 17: “Yes.”
chapterbody 18: “How can the sky be empty?”
chapterbody 19: “It similarly has no self-nature.”
chapterbody 21: “The sky has changes in the clouds; weather; light and dark; rain, wind, thunder, and lightning; and temperature. Everything changes; therefore, emptiness from no self-nature.”
chapterbody 23: Someone asked,
chapterbody 24: “Then how do you explain rocks?”
chapterbody 25: I answered,
chapterbody 26: “Rocks can be worn away by wind, rain and tremors. The blue sea becomes the mulberry field, the mulberry field becomes the blue sea. (Chinese idiom meaning ‘swift changes of the world.’) Thus, emptiness from no self-nature.”
chapterbody 27: “What is bright light?”
chapterbody 28: “Being immaculate is bright light.”
chapterbody 29: I clarified, “Bright light is the Buddha-nature.”
chapterbody 30: Someone asked, “Master Lu, do you recognize anything in this world that has self-nature?”
chapterbody 31: I answered,
chapterbody 32: “No. The [Heart Sutra] very clearly stated that there is no eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, or mind.”
chapterbody 33: Someone asked,
chapterbody 34: “How can you prove that there is none?”
chapterbody 36: “It’s proven in the [Diamond Sutra], which states that everything is in a combined form.”
chapterbody 37: (In this short chapter, the words were few, but they contain a deep meaning.)

chaptername: 044 Realize the Nature of Emptiness Through Meditation
chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “What does it prove by our realization on the nature of emptiness through meditation?”
chapterbody 3: I answered,
chapterbody 4: “The verification of the Buddha’s words.”
chapterbody 5: “What does this verification refer to?”
chapterbody 6: “Proofs of no self-arising, no arising because of others, no arising from the combination of self and others, and no arising without a cause.”
chapterbody 7: “These terms are difficult to understand. Please elaborate.”
chapterbody 8: I clarified, “Please place attention on the fourth phrase ‘not arising without a cause.’ All things have causes and conditions. It is because of causes and conditions do they come into existence. The fourth phrase proves: not arising from the self, not arising from others, and not arising from the combination of self and others.”
chapterbody 9: Someone asked,
chapterbody 10: “What else does this confirm?”
chapterbody 11: “Neither arising nor ceasing,
chapterbody 14: neither coming nor going.
chapterbody 15: These eight negations are proof of the nature of emptiness.”
chapterbody 17: “What do you mean by that?”
chapterbody 18: “The first phrase is about the intrinsic nature of birth and death. The second phrase is about the intrinsic nature of impermanence. The third phrase is about the intrinsic nature of non-attachment. The fourth phrase is about the intrinsic nature of Buddha-nature.”
chapterbody 19: “What else does this confirm?”
chapterbody 20: “I am empty. People are empty. Sentient beings are empty. Life is empty.”
chapterbody 21: “What is its deeper meaning?”
chapterbody 22: “Upon reflection of oneself, an individual is a composite; sentient beings are a compound; people are of a combined form; the concept of time is empty; space is also empty.”
chapterbody 23: “Can you give an example?”
chapterbody 24: “We feel that life is like a dream. When the time comes, the dream becomes empty. Life is a big illusion. It seems real, but, when it is time, we realize that it is only an illusion. Life is like a bubble. When we realize impermanence, the bubble bursts. Life is like a shadow. Looks like something is there, but actually it is just a shadow. It is not real. Therefore, this is all emptiness.”
chapterbody 26: “Dew and electricity are also analogies, right?”
chapterbody 27: “Correct.”
chapterbody 28: “Why is it that meditation can prove all of this?”
chapterbody 29: “During meditation in samadhi, one can come to understand that there is no I, on the inside and no I, on the outside. Therefore, one can prove it.”
chapterbody 30: I continued,
chapterbody 31: “One can also prove it by understanding nothing to gain, nothing to dwell on.”
chapterbody 32: Someone asked,
chapterbody 33: “If it is as you said, then doesn’t it make life very negative and without meaning?”
chapterbody 34: I answered,
chapterbody 36: Someone asked,
chapterbody 37: “How so?”
chapterbody 39: “If one wishes to understand the reasoning, one must be even more diligent. If one wishes to save sentient beings from attachments, one must be dedicated in leading people away from delusion and illusions. This calls for positivity. This kind of positivity is having bodhicitta. Bodhicitta is escaping suffering and attaining happiness.”

chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “I am confused about the course of samadhi. Please, Master Lu, would you clarify for us?”
chapterbody 3: I answered:
chapterbody 4: 1. First, one experiences happiness, dharma joy and enjoyment. This is the phenomenon of great bliss. I have previously talked about the sensation of qi, inner fire, and light drops passing through the channels.
chapterbody 5: 2. When one experiences great bliss, one will not think about the lesser worldly pleasures such as from:
chapterbody 6: Wealth
chapterbody 7: Sex
chapterbody 8: Fame
chapterbody 9: Food
chapterbody 10: Sleep
chapterbody 11: Therefore, one will veer away from greed, anger, ignorance, suspicion, arrogance and etc. One can avoid all the worldly filth.
chapterbody 12: 3. Moving forward a step, one will discover bright light and wind in the channels and become more and more wise. One can emit light. Deities can emit light. The rivers, mountains and the earth can emit light.
chapterbody 13: 4. The light one emits will gradually become more and more bright. This is the light of wisdom. Because the light is also the fire, it can burn off all of one’s afflictions.
chapterbody 14: 5. One’s wisdom fire can break through all obstacles. One has reached the state of “non-obstructions.” Everything then becomes the best arrangement.
chapterbody 15: 6. One will know that everything is empty. Self is empty. Others are empty. One knows clearly that: Time is empty. Space is empty. As long as the three times, as wide as the ten directions, as well as what’s in front of one’s eyes, all are empty. Emptiness appears.
chapterbody 16: 7. When one merges with the emptiness, there are no eyes, ears, tongue, body, or mind. No forms, sound, smell, taste, touch, or phenomena. No consciousness of the boundaries, no realm of consciousness. No ignorance, nor elimination of ignorance.
chapterbody 17: 8. At the same time, also begets:
chapterbody 18: Clairvoyance
chapterbody 19: Omnipresence
chapterbody 21: Being immovable
chapterbody 22: Obtainment of no arising and no perishing
chapterbody 23: 9. In the midst of arising, one is not being attached to any of it. In the midst of emptiness, one is not being attached to emptiness. Everything becomes unhindered. One can preach the Dharma by going with the flow. One’s bodhicitta is brought forth greatly.
chapterbody 24: 10. When the cultivator himself is one with the buddha, as if one with the void, dharma rain and ambrosia will rain down.
chapterbody 26: One reaches buddha’s preliminary awakening.
chapterbody 27: One attains buddha’s subtle awakening.
chapterbody 28: Someone asked,
chapterbody 29: “What Master Lu has stated, isn’t it the bliss, bright light, and emptiness of Vajrayana Buddhism?”
chapterbody 30: I answered,
chapterbody 31: “Not far from it!”
chapterbody 32: “The course is clear, but is it possible for these occurrences to overlap?”
chapterbody 33: “These ten types of occurrences in the course of samadhi can appear one by one, seemingly appear, mutually appear, or appear randomly. However, in summary, they would appear coarse to fine and then from fine to precise.”
chapterbody 34: “It’s incredible!”

chaptername: 046 The Experience of Enlightenment from Samadhi
chapterbody: omeone said,
chapterbody 2: “Samadhi is truly incredible. Please talk a little bit of your incredible experiences.”
chapterbody 3: I said:
chapterbody 4: Everything is a “combined form.”
chapterbody 5: My experience is of “oneness.”
chapterbody 6: For example:
chapterbody 7: Present, past and the future seem to be three distinct times, but I can string them together as one by going through the three times knowing what is in the past and knowing what’s in the future. I can even merge the past, present and future into one in Samadhi.
chapterbody 8: In addition:
chapterbody 9: In this sentient world, I can pass through the walls of every architectural building, even passing through earth, water, fire, wind and space.
chapterbody 10: Can enter large mountains.
chapterbody 11: Can enter large oceans.
chapterbody 13: Knowing that the Buddhalands are also of “one.”
chapterbody 15: Moreover:
chapterbody 16: Receiving spiritual union with one buddha is the same as receiving spiritual union with all of the buddhas, even as well as with all bodhisattvas, vajra protectors, dharma protectors, dakas and dakinis, and other heavenly beings.
chapterbody 17: Receive spiritual union with every deity.
chapterbody 18: One is the same as many.
chapterbody 19: Many is the same as one.
chapterbody 20: Even when the appearances are significantly different, they can still merge into one.
chapterbody 21: Such as:
chapterbody 22: The Buddha, Amitabha Buddha, and Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva merged to become Padmasambhava.
chapterbody 23: Amitabha Buddha, Manjushri Bodhisattva, and the Great King Yama all merged to become Yamantaka.
chapterbody 24: And so on and so forth.
chapterbody 26: Furthermore:
chapterbody 27: There are numerous buddha dharmas.
chapterbody 28: There are numerous Buddhist sects.
chapterbody 29: There are numerous cultivation rituals.
chapterbody 30: Yet, all the dharma’s ultimately have no difference.
chapterbody 31: They all are perfect.
chapterbody 32: They are all equal.
chapterbody 33: They have no difference.
chapterbody 34: In samadhi, there is no uniqueness and no self.
chapterbody 36: I have seen all the dharma realms. I have also seen one big dharma realm, which integrates all the dharma realms. It is an amalgamation of the embodied nature of dharma realms.
chapterbody 37: Very unusual.
chapterbody 38: At the same time, not unusual at all.
chapterbody 39: It feels the same.
chapterbody 42: In every large matter, one can understand the small matters.
chapterbody 43: In every small matter, one can understand the large matters.
chapterbody 44: One acquires the knowledge of all matters, big and small.
chapterbody 45: Omniscient.
chapterbody 46: In every phenomenon, acquiring the knowledge of all phenomena, and thus becoming all-knowing.
chapterbody 48: The eyes, ears, nose, tongue, body, mind, root mental consciousness, and alaya consciousness are the eight consciousnesses, which are of one consciousness.
chapterbody 49: One consciousness is also no consciousness.
chapterbody 50: Emptiness,
chapterbody 51: Host and associate exist in mutual harmony.
chapterbody 61: Buddha-nature,
chapterbody 62: Mutually shining and reflecting light on each other.

chaptername: 047 Can One be Possessed by Demons During Meditation?
chapterbody: omeone asked,
chapterbody 2: “Can one be possessed by demons during meditation?”
chapterbody 3: I answered, “Yes.” (Positively.)
chapterbody 4: “What can be done?”
chapterbody 5: “Needs the guidance of a real guru master to avoid going astray.”
chapterbody 6: “How many types of possession are there?”
chapterbody 7: “Many.”
chapterbody 8: “Master Lu, please clarify for us.”
chapterbody 9: I said:
chapterbody 10: When cultivating meditation, one must practice “armor protection,” use methods of “protection,” and cultivate “dharma protectors.”
chapterbody 11: One also needs to practice “boundary protection.”
chapterbody 12: To prevent the interference of demons.
chapterbody 14: When the Buddha was in Samadhi, three female demons of Papiyan (mara) appeared and used all kinds of methods to tempt him. Ordinary men would not have been as steadfast, most likely giving up their souls or suffering a great setback on their cultivation for having fallen into the trap of desire and being unable to recover.
chapterbody 16: Or:
chapterbody 17: During meditation, people nearby may try to prevent it, inciting one’s anger. This is interference by anger.
chapterbody 18: When anger arises, the fire burns through the forest of merits.
chapterbody 19: Loss of merits!
chapterbody 21: Or:
chapterbody 22: Cultivators of meditation can easily be arrogant. They believe themselves to be cultivators and believe others are stupid, ignorant, mundane, or inferior.
chapterbody 23: They think themselves saintly.
chapterbody 24: And others as commoners.
chapterbody 25: This type of attitude is caused by the “demon of arrogance” and one needs to have more compassion towards sentient beings.
chapterbody 26: When the demons arrive, they will tell you:
chapterbody 27: “You have reached enlightenment!”
chapterbody 28: “You are the reincarnation of a great buddha!”
chapterbody 29: “Your level is high above that of your master’s!”
chapterbody 30: Therefore, you become arrogant. (Turning into the demon of arrogance.)
chapterbody 31: Or:
chapterbody 32: Demons often attack using one’s weakest point.
chapterbody 34: For example:
chapterbody 35: While cultivating meditation, one has a vision of one’s dear wife committing adultery with one’s best friend. One then becomes very angry. Thus, one’s meditation is compromised.
chapterbody 36: Or:
chapterbody 37: A demon can transform in its psychic communication with one. During the day, during the night, in all hours of the day, one is in constant psychic communication; thus, interfering with one’s physical health and leading to insanity.
chapterbody 38: (There are a lot of people like this in the mental hospital.)
chapterbody 39: Also:
chapterbody 40: There are four kinds of demons
chapterbody 42: The demons of the five aggregates
chapterbody 43: The demons of death
chapterbody 44: The demons of heaven
chapterbody 45: There are also ten types of demons.
chapterbody 46: Including the above, plus the demons of karma, the demons of the mind, and the demons of attachments (attachment to virtues, attachment to dharma, attachment to worldly knowledge, and, worst of all, attachment to buddha).
chapterbody 48: “What can be done?”
chapterbody 49: I answered,
chapterbody 50: “Listen to the guidance of one’s guru master. ‘No self’ is the most wonderful method of breaking through these demons!”

Without their compassion, wisdom, blessings, and encouragement, this project would not have reached fruition.
chapterbody 3: In addition, we would like to acknowledge the diligent work put forth by the following volunteers on this project: the leader of US Daden, Master Lianseng; Nora Wang (translator), Sophia Yu (editor),
chapterbody 4: DJ Chang (editor), Thomas W. Chung, Ph.D. (proofreader and typesetter), and Renee Cordsen (publication). We would like to thank these dedicated and selfless volunteers who have contributed their time and effort to promote the works of Living Buddha Lian-sheng, and to support the publications of US Daden Culture.
chapterbody 6: We would also like to extend our sincere appreciation to all of the other volunteers who work behind the scenes, facilitating the translation process, and handling administrative responsibilities.
chapterbody 8: May all volunteers be blessed with immeasurable merits. May all sentient beings benefit from the ocean of wisdom.
